seo.title: Pre-Deploy Evals vs Runtime Checks for AI Agents
seo.description: Compare Pre-Deploy Evaluations and Runtime Checks for AI agent reliability. Understand trade-offs and when to use each approach.

content.intro: Ensuring AI agent reliability requires deliberate strategy around when and how you validate behavior. Pre-deploy evaluations and runtime checks represent two complementary philosophies — one focused on catching issues before they reach users, the other on monitoring and correcting behavior as it happens. Understanding the tradeoffs helps teams build more resilient AI systems.

approachA.name: Pre-Deploy Evaluations
approachA.description: Pre-deploy evaluations involve running structured test suites, benchmarks, and red-teaming exercises against an AI agent before it goes live. Teams assess model behavior across curated datasets and edge cases to establish a confidence baseline. This approach is deeply rooted in traditional software QA practices adapted for probabilistic systems.
strengths: Catches systematic failures and regressions before they impact real users
Provides documented evidence of safety and capability for compliance or stakeholder review
Enables controlled, repeatable testing across known edge cases and adversarial prompts
Encourages disciplined thinking about expected agent behavior and acceptance criteria
weaknesses: Cannot anticipate the full distribution of real-world inputs users will actually send
Evaluation datasets quickly become stale as user behavior and context evolve
Passing benchmarks does not guarantee safe behavior in novel or out-of-distribution scenarios
Provides no visibility or recourse once the agent is live and encountering unexpected situations

approachB.name: Runtime Checks
approachB.description: Runtime checks monitor, validate, and intervene in AI agent behavior continuously during live operation. Rather than relying solely on pre-deployment confidence, this approach treats every interaction as an opportunity to enforce guardrails, detect anomalies, and correct outputs before they reach end users. It shifts reliability from a gate into an ongoing process.
strengths: Catches failures that only emerge from real user inputs and production context
Enables immediate intervention — blocking, flagging, or correcting problematic outputs in real time
Provides continuous observability and audit trails across all live agent interactions
Adapts to evolving threats and misuse patterns without requiring a full redeploy cycle
weaknesses: Adds latency to the inference pipeline if checks are not carefully optimized
Requires ongoing maintenance of rules, policies, and detection logic as the agent evolves
May generate false positives that degrade user experience if thresholds are poorly calibrated
Does not replace the need for foundational model quality — it cannot fix deeply flawed base behavior

comparison.approachA: Before deployment, in a controlled testing environment
comparison.approachB: Continuously, during every live agent interaction

comparison.approachA: Limited to curated test cases and anticipated scenarios
comparison.approachB: Covers the full distribution of actual user inputs in production

comparison.approachA: Can block deployment but cannot act once the agent is live
comparison.approachB: Can block, flag, or correct outputs before they reach users in real time

comparison.approachA: Concentrated effort at release cycles; lighter ongoing burden
comparison.approachB: Requires continuous monitoring infrastructure and policy maintenance

comparison.approachA: Produces point-in-time reports useful for pre-launch sign-off
comparison.approachB: Generates continuous audit logs across all interactions for ongoing accountability

recommendation.heading: Which Should You Choose?
recommendation.summary: The most resilient AI systems use both approaches in tandem — pre-deploy evaluations to establish a quality baseline and catch known failure modes before launch, and runtime checks to handle the unpredictable reality of production. If you must prioritize one, runtime checks offer broader coverage of actual risk since real users will always surface behaviors your test suite never anticipated. Teams serious about reliability treat pre-deploy evaluation as the starting line, not the finish line.

cta.heading: Try Runtime Verification with Vex
cta.description: See how Vex adds a continuous layer of runtime guardrails to your AI agents — catching failures in production that evaluations alone will never find.
